An AI/ML Engineer often prefers denormalized/star-like models for feature generation and training because training pipelines frequently scan large fact tables and join many dimensions; pre-joining into a wide table (or maintaining a star schema optimized for analytics) reduces complex joins, lowers compute cost, and makes feature extraction more straightforward and reproducible (e.g., a fact_user_day table with user-day aggregates plus selected user attributes).
questions.explanation: Normalization is great for transactional systems and data integrity but can be expensive for analytics due to many joins. Denormalization (or star schemas) is common in warehouses because it optimizes read-heavy workloads—exactly what feature engineering and training jobs do. The key is balancing correctness (consistent dimensions) with performance and simplicity for downstream ML consumers.

questions.question_text:
You’re designing a feature store table for offline training. Which schema design best supports point-in-time correctness and avoids label leakage?

A. One wide table keyed only by user_id, overwritten daily with latest values
B. Feature values stored with (entity_id, feature_name, feature_value) only; no timestamps
C. Feature values stored with (entity_id, feature_timestamp, feature_columns...) and joined to labels using an as-of condition
D. Store only aggregated features without keeping any raw event time
options: A. One wide table keyed only by user_id, overwritten daily with latest values
B. Feature values stored with (entity_id, feature_name, feature_value) only; no timestamps
C. Feature values stored with (entity_id, feature_timestamp, feature_columns...) and joined to labels using an as-of condition
D. Store only aggregated features without keeping any raw event time
questions.explanation: Point-in-time correctness requires knowing what was known at prediction time. Storing features with timestamps (or validity ranges) allows an as-of join to labels (e.g., features at or before label_time). Overwriting with latest values (A) and omitting timestamps (B/D) can introduce leakage because future information may be used during training.

questions.question_text:
You need to store a large, append-only training dataset (hundreds of GB) used by Spark and Python training jobs. Which storage/layout choice is generally best and why?

A. One huge CSV file in object storage; simplest to read
B. Partitioned Parquet (or similar columnar format) in object storage, partitioned by date and/or key
C. A single JSON Lines file; flexible schema
D. Store everything only in a transactional OLTP database table
options: A. One huge CSV file in object storage; simplest to read
B. Partitioned Parquet (or similar columnar format) in object storage, partitioned by date and/or key
C. A single JSON Lines file; flexible schema
D. Store everything only in a transactional OLTP database table
questions.explanation: Columnar formats like Parquet enable predicate pushdown, column pruning, and efficient compression—critical for ML training where you often read subsets of columns/partitions. Partitioning reduces scan cost and improves parallelism. CSV/JSON (A/C) are larger and slower to scan, and OLTP databases (D) are typically not cost-effective for large-scale scans and distributed training workloads.

questions.question_text:
In an orchestration tool (Airflow/Dagster/Prefect), what is the most important reason to define explicit task dependencies for an ML feature pipeline?

A. It makes SQL queries run faster
B. It ensures the pipeline can be rerun deterministically and failures are isolated to specific stages
C. It automatically fixes data quality issues
D. It guarantees no late data arrives
options: A. It makes SQL queries run faster
B. It ensures the pipeline can be rerun deterministically and failures are isolated to specific stages
C. It automatically fixes data quality issues
D. It guarantees no late data arrives
questions.explanation: Orchestration dependencies encode the DAG of steps (extract → transform → validate → publish), enabling correct ordering, retries, and partial re-runs. This improves determinism and operability. Orchestration doesn’t inherently optimize SQL (A), fix data quality (C), or prevent late data (D); those require separate design choices (validation, watermarking, etc.).

questions.question_text:
You’re building a training dataset for a churn model from raw app events. Which pipeline design is most appropriate if the model is retrained daily, but events arrive continuously and can be late by up to 24 hours?

A. Pure streaming pipeline that updates the training dataset row-by-row in place
B. Daily batch snapshots with a lateness window (e.g., process D-1 with watermark) and immutable outputs
C. Manual CSV exports whenever product requests a retrain
D. Only compute features at inference time to avoid ETL
options: A. Pure streaming pipeline that updates the training dataset row-by-row in place
B. Daily batch snapshots with a lateness window (e.g., process D-1 with watermark) and immutable outputs
C. Manual CSV exports whenever product requests a retrain
D. Only compute features at inference time to avoid ETL
questions.explanation: Daily retraining typically benefits from batch snapshots because they are reproducible and easier to validate. Adding a lateness window/watermark accounts for late-arriving events while keeping outputs immutable (e.g., partitioned by date). Updating training data in place (A) makes reproducibility and debugging difficult. Manual exports (C) don’t scale. Computing everything at inference (D) can increase latency and does not solve training-time dataset construction.
